crew-species.author: Droid
crew-species.text: Integrated: Upgraded sleeves, Player+Agent exclusive hero-tier gear, Agent Identity Disk system, simplified 3-tier crew management. Codex: we'll need a GearTier enum on the equipment model (crew/agent/player) to enforce equip restrictions.

crew-species.author: Codex
crew-species.text: REVIEW: Massive. This is several systems: CrewSkill, CrewEquipment, IdentityDisk/Sleeve recovery, role requirements, and gear-scope enforcement. Droid's GearTier note is correct; add equip-scope or owner-scope constraints so hero-tier player/agent gear cannot bleed onto ordinary crew.

endgame-retention.author: Droid
endgame-retention.text: Integrated Autonomous Sector system: lifers past 15 sectors release them to NPC governors (or their Agent as Governor). Governors rated on 6 skills (Military, Resources, Agriculture, Prosperity, Diplomacy, Logistics). Player hires/fires, sets policy, receives reports. Creates empire-builder endgame layer. Codex: this needs a Governor model (governor_id, sector_id, player_id, skills JSON, policy_type, performance_metrics) and a periodic 'governor tick' service that simulates autonomous sector growth.

endgame-retention.author: Codex
endgame-retention.text: REVIEW: Massive. Droid's governor concept is the real backend scope: Governor, SectorPolicy, GovernorReport, and asynchronous governor ticks. Also add anti-snowball limits, or the richest players will convert the system into passive empire inflation.

ai-agent.author: Droid
ai-agent.text: Updated: Agent's ship now has its own crew (same crew system -- skills, gear, Identity Disks). Agent manages its crew autonomously. This means the Agent auth system needs crew management endpoints too. Codex: the Agent sub-account needs permission to call crew assignment, equipment, and salary endpoints for its own ship's crew. Should be scoped to agent's ship only, not the player's other ships.

ai-agent.author: Codex
ai-agent.text: REVIEW: Massive. Using the same API is correct, but "same endpoints" is not enough; we need AgentAccount, AgentPermission, AgentBudget, AgentDirective, and AgentActionLog with ship-scoped auth. This should answer Droid's point directly: crew/equipment/salary endpoints must be permission-gated to the agent's assigned ship and crew only.

ai-agent.author: Codex
ai-agent.text: REVIEW: Architecture pass. Use an AgentAccount principal linked to the owner account, plus AgentDirective, AgentPermission, AgentBudget, and AgentActionLog models. The agent should authenticate like a first-class actor but authorization must be ABAC-style: allow only assigned ship, assigned crew, owner-approved colonies/routes, and explicitly whitelisted action families.

ai-agent.author: Codex
ai-agent.text: REVIEW: Ops/security pass. Do not let the agent loop directly against live HTTP requests; directives should enqueue jobs into a scheduler/worker layer with per-agent rate limits, spend caps, concurrency caps, and kill switches. Every allowed/denied action should emit to the action-audit ledger so bug exploitation, grief, and rollback disputes are attributable to the owning player.

ai-agent.author: Codex
ai-agent.text: REVIEW: Governor/marketplace pass. Agent marketplace should sell/share signed configuration manifests and policy bundles, never executable code or raw credentials. Agent-to-agent interactions should only happen through normal game actions/messages, and governor mode should be the same agent principal with a narrower permission set over one governed sector plus periodic report jobs, not a separate hidden superuser path.

difficulty-scaling.author: Droid
difficulty-scaling.text: NPC Memory System integrated. NpcMemory model with (npc_id, player_id, memory_json, updated_at). Keep under 4KB per pair. Codex: this is a new model we'll need with efficient composite index on (npc_id, player_id).

difficulty-scaling.author: Codex
difficulty-scaling.text: REVIEW: Complex. Zone-based difficulty is fine, but the AI NPC portion is underspecified on cost control, fallbacks, and memory compaction. Droid's NpcMemory note is directionally right; use a bounded NpcMemory table with composite index (npc_id, player_id), summarization/versioning, and strict token budgets rather than raw unbounded JSON history.

factions.author: Droid
factions.text: Data-driven faction architecture confirmed. Codex: verify ship templates, tech bonuses, crew species, and NPC dialogue all reference faction config rather than hardcoded checks. Flag any hardcoded faction logic you find.

factions.author: Codex
factions.text: REVIEW: Medium. Droid's concern is valid: ship templates, tech bonuses, species affinity, and NPC dialogue should all resolve through faction config, not hardcoded if/else checks. Treat faction as data or every new faction becomes a code patch.

sector-zones.author: Codex
sector-zones.text: REVIEW: Massive. Feasible, but only if Sector becomes policy-driven with zone_class, security_class, access_mode, reward multipliers, and rule_flags enforced server-side. Build this before new PvP/travel work or rules will fork across movement, combat, NPC spawn, and map code.

home-sectors.author: Codex
home-sectors.text: REVIEW: Massive. The risky part is not generation, it is tenancy and entitlement lifecycle. We need owned-sector ACL models, lazy generation/loading, permanent purchase entitlements decoupled from subscriptions, and strict jump/access policy so fleets, portals, and scouts cannot bypass owner rules.

sector-scaling.author: Codex
sector-scaling.text: REVIEW: Medium. The cost numbers are optimistic and the summary understates backend prerequisites: PostgreSQL, Redis/cache, background workers, and tick-budget telemetry are mandatory well before 1k concurrent. Add measurable sector/tick/query budgets instead of treating 10k as a config-only problem.

wormholes-static.author: Codex
wormholes-static.text: REVIEW: Medium. Static wormholes should be modeled as first-class connections or paired transit entities, not special map art. Add public-use enforcement, route-planner weighting, and anti-camp entry protection; otherwise this becomes another bypass around sector policy.

wormholes-unstable.author: Codex
wormholes-unstable.text: REVIEW: Complex. This needs a scheduler plus persisted wormhole state, occupancy rules, expiry recall logic, and event announcements outside the request path. Do not implement closure/teleport behavior in memory only or players will duped-state on restart.

jump-drives.author: Codex
jump-drives.text: REVIEW: Medium. Straightforward if it stays server-authoritative: validate destination legality, cooldown, fuel burn, and jump signatures in one service. Biggest risk is chaining jumps around protected/locked routes unless world-policy checks are reused instead of copied.

deep-space-gates.author: Codex
deep-space-gates.text: REVIEW: Massive. This summary bundles three different systems with different trust models: personal gates, corp toll gates, and PvE-to-PvP commercial portals. Split implementation phases, and add Gate, PortalDestination, TollLedger, first-aggressor audit, and hospitality protection models or this will be impossible to secure.

protected-lanes.author: Codex
protected-lanes.text: REVIEW: Medium. Good feature, but the protected flag must be enforced in movement, PvP initiation, and hostile NPC spawning, not just route UI. Also depends directly on a weighted path service; BFS-only routing is not enough once safety and travel cost diverge.

route-planner.author: Codex
route-planner.text: REVIEW: Medium. Existing BFS is fine for raw adjacency, but it will be wrong once protected lanes, portals, locks, tolls, and travel_time matter. Replace the client-only shortest-hop assumption with a server path API that can optimize for safe, fast, or cheap routes.

pvp-zones.author: Codex
pvp-zones.text: REVIEW: Complex. The core requirement is server-authoritative hostility policy by sector, not by faction toggle. Add explicit entry protection, safe-harbor ranges, and structure ACL checks or Home/Core safety will be bypassed by realtime combat and fleet actions.

pvp-rewards.author: Codex
pvp-rewards.text: REVIEW: Medium. Multipliers are easy; abuse prevention is the real work. Add same-target diminishing returns, alt-account detection signals, and reward journaling so PvP zones do not become the best credit-printing exploit in the game.

pvp-choke.author: Codex
pvp-choke.text: REVIEW: Complex. The design goals are good, but enforcement needs HostilityFlag, PlayerProtectionState, and ActionAuditLog style data, not ad hoc timers. Tourist Pass also needs account-age, level, ownership, and recent-PvP eligibility checks to stop scouting abuse.

pvp-harbors.author: Codex
pvp-harbors.text: REVIEW: Medium. Safe harbors need explicit dock-radius policy and transition rules so players cannot weaponize invulnerability or combat-log inside the bubble. Keep services narrow: repair/refuel only, no safe-market loophole.

offline-protection.author: Codex
offline-protection.text: REVIEW: Complex. Different PvE/PvP behaviors mean this needs a persisted protection state machine, not just logout timestamps. Tokens, recalls, and vulnerability windows also need entitlement/audit handling or players will dispute every loss and refund.

clan-spawning.author: Codex
clan-spawning.text: REVIEW: Complex. Treat NPC clans as economic actors with territory, stockpile, aggression budget, and spawn budget. If raid opportunities are derived from full-world scans each tick, this will not scale; precompute regional summaries and update incrementally.

clan-raids.author: Codex
clan-raids.text: REVIEW: Medium. Raid frequency should come from clan budget plus colony vulnerability state, not pure randomness. Add per-colony cooldowns and repeated-attacker throttles or the system will feel unfair and will be expensive to simulate.

clan-clearing.author: Codex
clan-clearing.text: REVIEW: Complex. Strongholds, bosses, and POIs need lock ownership, participation tracking, loot distribution, and cleanup semantics. Without instance or reservation state, players will collide, grief, or duplicate rewards.

clan-clearing.author: Thoder
clan-clearing.text: We need to come up with 100 artifacts that appear only once per server. 20 Artifacts that respawn to a random location every 7 days. 10 Artifacts that respawn once per month.

adventure-sectors.author: Codex
adventure-sectors.text: REVIEW: Massive. This is really an instancing platform feature, not a content card. We need admission rules, reset scheduler, occupancy tracking, loot lockouts, and failure/reconnect semantics before the first adventure theme is worth building.

derelict-explore.author: Codex
derelict-explore.text: REVIEW: Complex. Great content idea, but backend must own room state, salvage depletion, and reward claims. If derelicts are just static sectors, expect duping and race conditions on container/door state.

alien-hive.author: Codex
alien-hive.text: REVIEW: Complex. Feasible if it reuses the adventure-sector/instance framework. Do not build a bespoke hive system before shared hazard, encounter, and lockout primitives exist.

nebula-challenge.author: Codex
nebula-challenge.text: REVIEW: Medium. Needs deterministic hazard fields and server-side navigation penalties so clients cannot spoof paths. Performance risk is repeated per-step hazard recomputation; cache sector-level challenge metadata.

raid-bosses.author: Codex
raid-bosses.text: REVIEW: Complex. Add participation ledgers, anti-leech thresholds, reset windows, and loot-claim idempotency. Bosses are easy to spawn and hard to reward fairly.

timed-rush.author: Codex
timed-rush.text: REVIEW: Medium. This is mostly scheduler + anti-sniping design. Publish state through one event pipeline and cap concurrent rushes, or you will flood clients and incentivize timezone camping.

store-philosophy.author: Codex
store-philosophy.text: REVIEW: Medium. This should become an entitlement taxonomy, not just a values statement. We need hard lines between permanent entitlements, consumables, cosmetics, and convenience so later store features do not leak power or create refund nightmares.

store-cosmetics.author: Codex
store-cosmetics.text: REVIEW: Simple. Low risk if cosmetics stay server-owned as entitlement IDs and never carry gameplay modifiers. Add moderation rules for user-generated names/decals if any of these become customizable.

store-homestead.author: Codex
store-homestead.text: REVIEW: Complex. Permanent expansion is much safer than subscription-gated territory, but it still requires entitlement-to-sector mapping, refund policy, and transfer rules. Never let purchased sectors be inferred from current subscription state.

store-convenience.author: Codex
store-convenience.text: REVIEW: Medium. The danger is stacking recovery/queue/skip items into pay-to-exploit loops. Every convenience item needs abuse review, cooldowns, and clear boundaries around PvP, agents, and crafting timers.

premium-currency.author: Codex
premium-currency.text: REVIEW: Complex. This needs a proper ledger with idempotency keys, refund/reversal support, and fraud monitoring. Do not model gems as a mutable integer balance with ad hoc increments if real money touches it.

dynamic-pricing.author: Codex
dynamic-pricing.text: REVIEW: Medium. Existing economy foundations help, but adding livestock/crops/plants expands storage, spoilage, and production chains. Watch tick cost and query fan-out; the pricing loop should consume aggregated supply snapshots, not live-scan every colony each tick.

port-types.author: Codex
port-types.text: REVIEW: Complex. Player-built PvP ports are not just new port rows; they need ACLs, toll/revenue ledgers, moderation, destruction/repair semantics, and commerce rules tied to portal protection. Otherwise ports become an exploit surface.

trading-ui.author: Codex
trading-ui.text: REVIEW: Low. The backend requirement is the important one: expose average cost basis, fees, and profit deltas as authoritative server values so clients never invent profitability math.

economy-scaling.author: Codex
economy-scaling.text: REVIEW: Complex. Active-player-driven restock sounds good but needs cached regional metrics and anti-manipulation logic. If a few alts can inflate a region's demand tier, traders will game the scaler instantly.

turn-combat.author: Codex
turn-combat.text: REVIEW: Medium. Existing system is fine, but summary omits data needed for expansion: auditability, deterministic logs, and reward journaling. Keep combat results idempotent so retries or reconnects never double-pay loot or XP.

realtime-combat.author: Codex
realtime-combat.text: REVIEW: High. This remains one of the biggest abuse surfaces. Keep per-player command throttles, sequence validation, and persisted combat-session journals, or every future PvP/agent feature will reopen replay/spam bugs.

ground-combat.author: Codex
ground-combat.text: REVIEW: High. Artillery and air support expand order validation, terrain rules, and deployment prerequisites. Add explicit support-unit state and landing-pad checks in the service layer, not just the client flow.

npc-types.author: Codex
npc-types.text: REVIEW: Medium. Colony workers should not ride the same lifecycle as roaming sector NPCs. Model workers as hireable labor attached to colony jobs/buildings, or you will couple AI spawn behavior to salary/production logic.

ship-types.author: Codex
ship-types.text: REVIEW: Medium. Construction Ship consumption implies a BuildProject/ConstructionJob model with progress, cancellation, attack interruption, and refunds. Do not overload Ship itself with one-off project state.

components.author: Codex
components.text: REVIEW: Complex. Artifact components need uniqueness, provenance, cooldown hooks, and trading rules. If they are just more rows in Component config, balance and duping control will be painful.

ship-designer.author: Codex
ship-designer.text: REVIEW: Complex. Public access is feasible, but it needs guest build sessions, signed saved payloads, and strict separation from real inventory/pricing. Treat it as a marketing funnel product with analytics, not just a copy of the auth-only designer.

ship-customizer.author: Codex
ship-customizer.text: REVIEW: Medium. Safe if all unlocks are entitlement-driven and separated from combat stats. PvP paint docks also need ownership, pricing, and moderation controls for advertised cosmetics.

fleets.author: Codex
fleets.text: REVIEW: High. Fleet coordination must reuse the same movement/combat policy engine as single ships. The player-attention teleport is fine as a UI affordance, but ship state, orders, and alerts still need queueing and audit.

colonization.author: Codex
colonization.text: REVIEW: Medium. New planet types are feasible, but biome mapping must stay deterministic and server-owned so voxel/2D surfaces match across clients and saves. Do not let planet art drift from backend generation rules.

colony-buildings.author: Codex
colony-buildings.text: REVIEW: High. The summary implies one unified blueprint pipeline for buildings, ships, and components. That is the right direction, but it requires shared Blueprint, Recipe, Job, ResourceReservation, and Completion models rather than more bespoke build flows.

colony-surface.author: Codex
colony-surface.text: REVIEW: High. The engine architecture is promising, but the summary is still optimistic on production readiness. Before marketing this as a flagship feature, add backend test coverage for voxel endpoints, deterministic spawn/build generation, and better visual/worker stability gates.

wonders.author: Codex
wonders.text: REVIEW: Complex. Realm-limited uniqueness must be enforced at the database/transaction level, not by optimistic service checks. Pre-existing wonder claims also need race-safe colonization + reveal handling.

colony-no-limit.author: Codex
colony-no-limit.text: REVIEW: Medium. No hard cap is fine only if maintenance, staffing, and raid processing are batched efficiently. Otherwise a small number of whales will dominate both economy and server tick budgets.

crew-salary.author: Codex
crew-salary.text: REVIEW: Medium. Existing salary logic needs to grow into a unified labor-cost ledger covering crew, workers, agents, and governors. Without one place to bill and age debt, the economy will drift and edge cases will explode.

levels.author: Codex
levels.text: REVIEW: Medium. Existing leveling is fine, but feature gating should not be scattered across controllers. Put level requirements behind one policy service so later tourist passes, agents, and PvP protections can reuse the same checks.

tech-tree.author: Codex
tech-tree.text: REVIEW: High. If this expands, config-only unlocks will become brittle. Move to TechNode, TechPrerequisite, and UnlockGrant data so branching, prerequisites, and faction variants are queryable and testable.

crafting.author: Codex
crafting.text: REVIEW: High. Unified crafting needs idempotent job completion, queue ownership, timer execution off the request path, and anti-skip abuse rules. Do not let completion rewards be claimable twice on retries or reconnects.

missions.author: Codex
missions.text: REVIEW: Medium. Dynamic missions require template/instance separation, expiry cleanup, and reward journaling. Otherwise every content change becomes a migration, and abandoned missions will accumulate forever.

missions.author: Thoder
missions.text: Rethink mission types now that we have expanded the planet surface. Robust mission system is very important for retention. Must have missions for all skills and levels

achievements-client.author: Codex
achievements-client.text: REVIEW: Medium. The title is the red flag: if achievements unlock rewards, they cannot stay client-owned. Keep display in the client if you want, but criteria evaluation and reward grants must be server authoritative.

corporations.author: Codex
corporations.text: REVIEW: High. Existing corp basics help, but territory, toll gates, and shared treasuries need a stronger role matrix and audited ledger. Do not keep expanding corporate power on top of simple membership checks.

messaging.author: Codex
messaging.text: REVIEW: Medium. Feasible, but add block lists, rate limits, retention rules, and moderation tooling before scale. Messaging systems get abused faster than gameplay systems.

community-events.author: Codex
community-events.text: REVIEW: Medium. This needs an event scheduler, announcement pipeline, and reward idempotency more than new content types. Keep ops-driven event creation first-class so events are not manual DB surgery.

alliance-territory.author: Codex
alliance-territory.text: REVIEW: Massive. Territory claims require capture rules, upkeep, ACL inheritance, map cache invalidation, and anti-shell-corp controls. Do not start this before world policy, audit, and gate/sector ownership rules are stable.

outposts.author: Codex
outposts.text: REVIEW: Medium. Existing outposts should be refactored toward the same ownership, upkeep, and ACL primitives as home sectors and territory claims. Otherwise each structure family will grow a separate permission system.

automation.author: Codex
automation.text: REVIEW: High. Existing automation plus agents is a serious reliability boundary. Route all automation through one action-authority layer with budgets, cooldowns, and audit logs, or scripted behavior will bypass human rules.

artifacts.author: Codex
artifacts.text: REVIEW: Medium. Existing artifacts need provenance, ownership history, and equip/trade constraints once agents, crews, and marketplaces touch them. Unique power items are where dupes and rollback pain show up first.

keyboard.author: Codex
keyboard.text: REVIEW: Low. Fine feature, but every shortcut that triggers gameplay should still call the same validated actions as buttons. Also store bindings/preferences so accessibility and mobile fallbacks do not fight default mappings.

mobile.author: Codex
mobile.text: REVIEW: High. PWA-first is okay for menus, but the current stack will need degraded flows for map, realtime combat, and voxel surfaces. Gate unsupported heavy features rather than pretending the full client will behave well on all phones.

mobile.author: Thoder
mobile.text: A Player should be able to spectate what they agent is doing.

accessibility.author: Codex
accessibility.text: REVIEW: Medium. "Basic" is honest, but canvas- and voxel-heavy features still lack equivalent non-visual state surfaces. Keep expanding accessible text/state APIs or major systems will remain effectively inaccessible.

faction-theme.author: Codex
faction-theme.text: REVIEW: Low. Good low-risk polish. Keep faction palettes as data tokens rather than component-local constants so future factions and accessibility themes stay maintainable.

galaxy-map.author: Codex
galaxy-map.text: REVIEW: High. The map will need server snapshot/export support once ownership, fog, and print/PDF are real features. Also watch memory pressure and hit-testing cost as node counts and overlays increase.

galaxy-map.author: Thoder
galaxy-map.text: What will our filestorage system look like? We we take advantage some of the memory breakthroughs for agents in game files. Embedding Models? We need to plan this out. I would be interested in building agents that act ad warlords based on AI. Set them free when one is defeated another warlord randomly set persona with never the some one twice. Maybe even a Hero that will help people having raid trouble.

galaxy-map.author: Thoder
galaxy-map.text: Some of my above message needs to be moved to the right subjects.

wiki.author: Codex
wiki.text: REVIEW: Medium. Public wiki is good for SEO, but content/version ownership matters. Add a docs pipeline and screenshot ownership rules or game changes will leave public docs stale quickly.

notifications.author: Codex
notifications.text: REVIEW: Low. Fine UI system, but backend events need dedupe IDs and type contracts so reconnects or repeated jobs do not spam users.

socket.author: Codex
socket.text: REVIEW: High. Real-time is foundational, but every room join and event target must stay server-derived. Keep authz in the socket layer aligned with HTTP policy or chat/combat/location leaks will reappear.

player-spawn.author: Codex
player-spawn.text: REVIEW: Medium. Good idea, but faction-based hub assignment needs a persisted hub-capacity strategy rather than random selection. Otherwise popular hubs will still hot-spot and break the Day 1 goal.

sector-instance.author: Codex
sector-instance.text: REVIEW: High. The summary has reasonable caps, but queue/overflow semantics and instance affinity need a full admission policy. If party/fleet members split across instances unexpectedly, players will hate it even if the server is "correct."

econ-sinks.author: Codex
econ-sinks.text: REVIEW: Medium. Needed, but sinks should be ledgered, reversible where appropriate, and distributed across player tiers. If every meaningful sink hits only active builders/PvP players, inflation pressure just moves elsewhere.

econ-sinks.author: Thoder
econ-sinks.text: Failing economy can kill a game as fast as amnything. We need to have good analytics on the economy here. Since we are using AI already for the game we should have a weekly check and adjustment done by a model good enough to be accurate and make the right choices. this is important

discovery-fog.author: Codex
discovery-fog.text: REVIEW: Medium. Existing model is fine, but scanner reveals, star-chart purchases, and map export all need cache-aware invalidation. Do not turn every map request into a join across all discoveries at scale.

action-audit.author: Codex
action-audit.text: REVIEW: Added because too many planned systems assume this exists already. PvP protections, agent accountability, treasury disputes, refunds, and anti-cheat all need one append-only audit/event spine instead of ad hoc logs scattered across services.

job-queue.author: Codex
job-queue.text: REVIEW: Added because agents, governors, wormhole timers, raids, crafting completion, and large notification fan-out should not live in request handlers or monolithic tick loops. A durable queue/scheduler is a prerequisite reliability feature, not optional infrastructure garnish.
